name: Hugh Lawson
abstract: Hugh Lawson (March 12, 1935 – March 11, 1997), was an American jazz pianist from Detroit who worked with Yusef Lateef for more than 10 years. Inspired by Bud Powell, Hampton Hawes and Bill Evans, Lawson first gained recognition for his work with Lateef from 1957 onwards.

about_quotes.speaker: Louis Hayes
about_quotes.evidence: So that was Yusef and Curtis Fuller was playing trombone and Hugh Lawson piano, and Ernie Farrell was the bassist. And I went in with them
about_quotes.doc_title: Louis Hayes interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Yusef Lateef
about_quotes.evidence: Uh it was Ernie Farrell on bass, Hugh Lawson–piano. Will Austin–bass, Louis Hayes–drums.
about_quotes.doc_title: Yusef Lateef — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: I got Hugh Lawson . He played piano with me. And had the same group.

about_quotes.speaker: Curtis Fuller
about_quotes.evidence: I wrote a song for him because he was my age and stuff... I used to call Hugh Lawson, you know? Hugo is his name but, yeah. So, it's a cross because we used to always make fun of that Igor stuff, you know with Frankenstein. So, we called him Hugo. It was written for him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Curtis Fuller — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jones Hank
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer lists Hugh Lawson as part of a group ('coming after you Flanagan Barry Harris, Roland Hana. Then, after them, Hugh Lawson and Kirk.') but the interviewee does not confirm a personal relationship, only acknowledging the list.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jones Hank — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Barry Harris
about_quotes.evidence: Hugh Lawson was part of it. ... All these people came – were in my house
about_quotes.doc_title: Barry Harris — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
